title: Effective attraction by repulsion
authors: Rosalba Garcia-Millan, Luca Cocconi, Ziluo Zhang, Marius Bothe, Letian Chen, Zigan Zhen, Gunnar Pruessner
abstract: Repulsive self-propelled particles tend to cluster, leading to Motility-Induced Phase Separation (MIPS). By analogy with equilibrium phase separation, the onset of MIPS has been associated with a transition to effective attraction between particles. Using an exact microscopic theory, we quantify the emergence of effective attraction in a minimal model: two soft run-and-tumble particles in a periodic domain. We show that, as repulsion increases, the leading-order behaviour is that of effective repulsion, while effective attraction emerges as a higher-order contribution to the renormalisation of the pair potential.
summary: This paper provides a microscopic theoretical explanation for how purely repulsive active particles can exhibit effective attraction, a key driver of phase separation in active matter. By using field-theoretic renormalization, the authors show that attraction emerges as a higher-order effect of the repulsion. This bridges the gap between individual particle dynamics and macroscopic phase separation phenomena.
main_problem: Investigating the microscopic origin of effective attraction in Motility-Induced Phase Separation (MIPS) and clarifying how purely repulsive interactions between active particles lead to particle accumulation.
main_result: The study demonstrates that while the leading-order effect of increasing repulsion is effective repulsion, higher-order renormalization effects create an emergent effective attraction. This provides a microscopic basis for the equilibrium-like phase separation analogy in active matter.
method: The authors use a bottom-up microscopic theory based on Doi-Peliti Field Theory (DPFT) and a path-integral approach, employing a perturbation expansion and Feynman diagrams to calculate loop corrections to the interaction vertices.
model_or_system: A minimal model of two soft run-and-tumble particles (RTPs) in a one-dimensional periodic domain, interacting via a repulsive Yukawa potential and undergoing stochastic reorientation (tumbling).
key_observables: Two-point correlation function, compressibility factor (S1), joint particle density, effective potential (W_eff), and the distinction between jamming distance (x_J) and accumulation distance (x_A).
important_parameters: Normalized repulsion coupling (nu-bar), Péclet number (Pe) representing activity, normalized interaction length (xi-bar), and normalized reorientation rate (gamma-bar).
main_assumptions: The model is restricted to a minimal 1D two-particle system and assumes a Yukawa potential for the soft interactions.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows a schematic of the particles, a plot of compressibility vs. repulsion strength showing a crossover, and a phase diagram; Figure 2 displays joint particle density profiles for varying repulsion and activity levels.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of MIPS and the equilibrium analogy, describes the 1D two-particle model and field-theoretic framework, presents the perturbative results showing the emergence of attraction, and concludes by discussing the implications for higher-dimensional MIPS.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) Two soft RTPs in a periodic domain (arrows indi- cate orientation). (b) Compressibility factor S1 = ⟨cos(k1x)⟩, as a function of repulsion ¯ν, showing the crossover from effec- tive repulsion (S1 < 0) to effective attraction (S1 > 0). An- alytical results (line) and numerical results (symbols) agree. Parameters: Dx = 2, L = 20, ¯ξ = 0.01, Pe = 2, ¯γ = 0.02. (c) Compressibility as a function of ¯ν and Pe. Blue indicates effective repulsion, red indicates effective attraction. Param- eters: Dx = 1, L = 20, ¯ξ = 0.02, ¯γ = 0.02.

FIG. 2. Joint particle density P(x1, x2) as a function of rela- tive particle distance varying (a) repulsion ¯ν and (b) activity Pe. Parameters: Dx = 0.5, L = 20, ¯γ = 0.008, ¯ξ = 0.01, (a) Pe = 20, and (b) ¯ν = 10.

FIG. 3. (a) Stationary joint two-point correlation functions P++ and P−+. Parameters: Dx = 0.5, L = 20, ¯ξ = 0.01, ¯ν = 10, Pe = 20, ¯γ = 0.008. (b) Accumulation distance xA (dots and lines), the maximum of P−+ as shown in (a), and jamming distance xJ (dashed), Eq. (4), as a function of ν. Dots indicate effective repulsion (S1 < 0) and solid lines indicate effective attraction (S1 > 0). Parameters: Dx = 1, L = 20, ¯ξ = 0.01, ¯γ = 0.01. (c) Effective potential Weff = −ln(2L2P−+) between right- and left-moving particles. Parameters: Dx = 1, L = 20, ¯ν = 5, ¯ξ = 0.01, ¯γ = 0.01.
